_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against AAD 2023-2024 Atopic Dermatitis Guidelines (Sidbury / Davis JAAD), EuroGuiDerm 2022 (Wollenberg), Langan Lancet 2020 AD seminar, GBD 2017 Laughter et al BJD 2021, SOLO-1/SOLO-2 dupilumab pivotal trials (Simpson NEJM 2016), and Guttman-Yassky JAMA Dermatol 2020 upadacitinib data. Drug names and doses cross-checked against UpToDate Lexicomp and FDA prescribing information.

h1Title: Eczema: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Eczema is a chronic, relapsing inflammatory skin disease driven by a defective epidermal barrier and a type 2 immune response that produces intense itch, dry skin, and scaling rashes. The term most often refers to atopic dermatitis, the dominant form, which affects roughly 20% of children and 2-10% of adults in high-income countries and an estimated 204 million people worldwide. The hallmark is unbearable itching that disturbs sleep, with red papulovesicles, oozing patches, and over time thickened lichenified plaques on the flexural surfaces of the elbows, knees, neck, and ankles. Without consistent treatment, the disease drives skin infections, growth impairment, anxiety, and depression, and predicts later food allergy, asthma, and hay fever — the atopic march. Modern therapy now ranges from ceramide moisturizers and topical corticosteroids to biologics such as dupilumab that achieve clear or almost-clear skin in roughly half of moderate-severe patients within 16 weeks.

keyStats.prevalence: ~20% of children and 2-10% of adults in developed countries (Langan Lancet 2020)
keyStats.demographics: Onset before age 5 in 85% of pediatric cases; persists into adulthood in 30-40%
keyStats.avgAge: First flare typically between 3 and 6 months of age; adult-onset disease is recognized in 1-3%
keyStats.globalCases: ~204 million people globally with atopic dermatitis (GBD 2017)

definition: Eczema is the everyday term for atopic dermatitis (ICD-10: L20), the most common chronic inflammatory dermatosis worldwide, with the broader ICD-10 category L30 covering other and unspecified dermatitis subtypes. Pathologically it is a barrier-immune disease: filaggrin loss-of-function mutations (carried by 10-50% of moderate-to-severe patients of European ancestry), ceramide depletion, and increased transepidermal water loss break the stratum corneum, while a T-helper 2 (Th2) skewed immune response releases interleukin-4, interleukin-13, and interleukin-31 — the cytokines that drive itch, lichenification, and Staphylococcus aureus overgrowth. Disease severity is graded with validated tools such as the Eczema Area and Severity Index (EASI), the Scoring Atopic Dermatitis (SCORAD) index, and the validated Investigator Global Assessment for atopic dermatitis (vIGA-AD). Eczema is also an umbrella label for related dermatitides — contact dermatitis (irritant and allergic), dyshidrotic eczema, nummular eczema, seborrheic dermatitis, and stasis dermatitis — which share scaling, erythema, and pruritus but differ in trigger, distribution, and mechanism. The condition is managed primarily by dermatologists, with pediatricians, allergists, and primary care heavily involved given how often it overlaps with asthma, allergic rhinitis, and food allergy in the atopic march.

typesClassification.description: The dominant clinical entity meant by 'eczema'. Chronic, relapsing, with infant facial and extensor involvement that shifts to flexural creases in older children and adults. Strongly associated with personal or family atopy.

Localized eczematous reaction at sites of skin contact with chemicals, metals (nickel), fragrances, or plants. Allergic contact dermatitis is delayed-type hypersensitivity confirmed by patch testing; irritant disease is non-immunologic.
Recurrent crops of intensely itchy, deep-seated vesicles on the palms, sides of fingers, and soles. Often flares with sweating, nickel exposure, or stress.
Coin-shaped (numular) plaques on the extremities and trunk, most common in adults and frequently mistaken for tinea or psoriasis. Linked to dry skin and barrier disruption.
Greasy yellow scale on the scalp, eyebrows, nasolabial folds, and chest. Driven by an inflammatory response to Malassezia yeast on sebum-rich skin. Cradle cap is the infant form.
Lower-leg eczema in adults with chronic venous insufficiency. Pruritic, hyperpigmented, scaly skin over the medial ankles that can progress to ulceration if untreated.

primarySymptoms: Intense, persistent itch (pruritus) that worsens at night and is severe enough to disturb sleep in roughly 60% of moderate-severe patients — the cardinal symptom and the one most strongly tied to quality-of-life impairment.
Dry, rough, scaly skin (xerosis) with reduced elasticity from increased transepidermal water loss, present even between flares.
Erythematous papules and papulovesicles that may weep clear fluid during acute flares, classically on the cheeks and scalp in infants.

Lichenification — thickened, leathery skin with exaggerated skin markings — over chronically scratched flexural surfaces such as the antecubital and popliteal fossae, neck, and ankles in older children and adults.
Excoriations, fissures, and crusting from repeated scratching, with frequent secondary bacterial infection (golden crust suggests Staphylococcus aureus impetiginization).
Age-dependent distribution: cheeks, scalp, and extensor surfaces in infants under 2; flexural creases of elbows, knees, wrists, and ankles in older children; hands, eyelids, and neck in adults.
Sleep disturbance, irritability, and daytime fatigue from nocturnal itch — measured on validated scales such as the Patient-Oriented Eczema Measure (POEM).
Pityriasis alba — ill-defined hypopigmented patches on the face after inflammation resolves, more visible on darker skin types.
Periorbital darkening (atopic shiners), Dennie-Morgan infraorbital folds, and a single transverse fold below the lower lip are classic atopic stigmata.
Burning, stinging, and pain in heavily fissured or infected areas, especially around the hands and feet during dyshidrotic flares.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistently dry, sensitive skin in an infant that flares with bath time, fragranced products, or new fabrics
Itchy red cheeks or scalp scale within the first 6 months of life, especially with a family history of atopic disease
Recurrent rough patches in the antecubital or popliteal creases that improve with moisturizer and worsen with soap or sweat
Frequent crops of small itchy vesicles on the sides of the fingers in adults working with water, detergents, or metals
Worsening eczema during winter months or after starting a new occupational exposure
emergencySigns: Sudden widespread painful punched-out vesicles, fever, and lymphadenopathy — eczema herpeticum, a dermatologic emergency requiring IV acyclovir
Rapidly spreading erythema with golden honey-coloured crusting and fever — extensive Staphylococcus aureus impetiginization or cellulitis needing oral or IV antibiotics
Generalized red, scaling, peeling skin involving more than 90% of the body surface (erythroderma) with chills and dehydration — admit for fluid balance and systemic therapy
Severe periorbital swelling, eye pain, photophobia, or visual change — possible secondary keratoconjunctivitis or HSV keratitis warranting same-day ophthalmology review

causes.cause: Filaggrin loss-of-function mutations and epidermal barrier failure
causes.description: Filaggrin is essential for stratum corneum integrity and natural moisturizing factor production. Loss-of-function variants are present in 10-50% of moderate-severe atopic dermatitis patients of European ancestry and confer a 3-4 fold increased risk. Even without filaggrin mutations, ceramide depletion and tight-junction abnormalities raise transepidermal water loss.

Interleukin-4 and interleukin-13 drive class switching to IgE, suppress antimicrobial peptides, and impair barrier repair. Interleukin-31 is the dominant itch cytokine. Blocking IL-4 receptor alpha with dupilumab reverses much of this signal and validates the pathway clinically.

causes.cause: Skin microbiome dysbiosis and Staphylococcus aureus overgrowth
causes.description: Staphylococcus aureus colonizes the skin of 70-90% of moderate-severe atopic dermatitis patients and rises further during flares. Its superantigens amplify Th2 inflammation, perpetuating the itch-scratch cycle. Restoring microbial diversity correlates with clinical improvement.

Soaps, detergents, fragrances, wool, dust mite, pet dander, pollen, cigarette smoke, hard water, sweat, and cold dry air all disturb the barrier or amplify inflammation. Each patient has a distinct trigger profile, identified through history and (where indicated) patch testing.

About 30% of infants with moderate-severe atopic dermatitis have IgE-mediated food allergy, most often to milk, egg, peanut, wheat, or soy. Food allergy is a comorbidity rather than the primary driver in most cases; broad elimination diets without clear evidence cause more harm than benefit.

causes.cause: Stress, sleep loss, and the itch-scratch cycle
causes.description: Psychological stress triggers neuropeptide release that lowers the itch threshold; nocturnal scratching damages barrier and seeds infection. Cognitive-behavioural and habit-reversal techniques meaningfully reduce flares in chronic disease.

riskFactors.factor: Family or personal history of atopy
riskFactors.description: A first-degree relative with eczema, asthma, or allergic rhinitis raises risk 2-3 fold. Concordance in monozygotic twins is roughly 75%, indicating strong heritability.
Heterozygotes have a 3-4 fold increased risk; homozygotes develop more severe, early-onset, and persistent disease, often with palmar hyperlinearity and ichthyosis vulgaris.

Infants with measurable transepidermal water loss in the first weeks of life have substantially higher rates of atopic dermatitis by age one. Daily emollient from birth may modestly reduce incidence in high-risk infants.

riskFactors.factor: Living in a developed or urban environment
riskFactors.description: Atopic dermatitis prevalence is highest in high-income, urban populations and lowest in rural agrarian settings — consistent with the hygiene and biodiversity hypotheses.
Calcium carbonate raises skin pH and barrier disruption; UK birth cohort data link hard water to a 1.5-fold higher infant eczema rate.

riskFactors.factor: Maternal smoking and prenatal pollution exposure
riskFactors.description: Both raise offspring eczema risk by roughly 15-20% per major cohort meta-analyses.

riskFactors.factor: Caesarean delivery and early antibiotic exposure
riskFactors.description: Both alter early microbial colonization and modestly raise atopic dermatitis incidence.

riskFactors.factor: Female sex in adolescence and adulthood
riskFactors.description: Infant boys are affected slightly more; after puberty, women carry higher prevalence and more hand and head-neck involvement.

riskFactors.factor: Occupations with wet work or chemical exposure
riskFactors.description: Healthcare, food service, hairdressing, cleaning, and metalworking are linked to chronic hand eczema rates of 10-30%.

riskFactors.factor: High maternal age and small-family size
riskFactors.description: Independent associations consistent with the hygiene hypothesis; reduced early-life microbial exposure correlates with higher atopic disease incidence.

affectedDemographics: US prevalence: 7.3% of adults and 10.7% of children meet criteria for active eczema (Silverberg, JAMA Dermatol)
Black and Asian children in the US and UK carry the highest pediatric prevalence and tend toward more severe, papular, follicular-pattern disease
Onset before age 5 in 85% of pediatric cases; symptoms persist into adulthood in 30-40% of childhood cases
Adult-onset atopic dermatitis is recognized in 1-3% of adults, often with hand, head, and neck distribution
Highest national rates reported from Northwestern Europe, Australia, and parts of Africa; lowest from rural China and India (ISAAC data)
diagnosisOverview: Eczema is a clinical diagnosis — no single laboratory test confirms it. Dermatologists and primary-care clinicians use the UK Working Party refinement of the Hanifin-Rajka criteria: an itchy skin condition plus three or more of (1) history of flexural involvement, (2) personal or family history of atopy, (3) generally dry skin in the last year, (4) onset before age 2, or (5) visible flexural dermatitis. Severity is graded with the Eczema Area and Severity Index (EASI), SCORAD, the Patient-Oriented Eczema Measure (POEM), and the vIGA-AD. Patch testing is indicated when distribution suggests allergic contact dermatitis (eyelids, hands, perianal area, isolated regions that flare with specific products) or when established atopic dermatitis fails to respond to standard therapy. Skin biopsy is rarely needed but is performed to exclude cutaneous T-cell lymphoma, psoriasis, or other mimickers in atypical or treatment-resistant adult cases. Total serum IgE is elevated in 70-80% of moderate-severe patients but is non-specific. Targeted food-allergy testing (specific IgE or skin-prick) is reserved for infants with moderate-severe eczema and a convincing history of immediate reactions — broad untargeted panels generate false positives and drive harmful elimination diets. The decisive differentials to keep in mind are scabies in any new-onset severely itchy eruption (especially in households with multiple itchy members), psoriasis when plaques are well-demarcated with silvery scale, contact dermatitis when distribution maps to an exposure, and cutaneous lymphoma in long-standing adult disease that fails escalating therapy.

diagnosticTests.test: Clinical examination with Hanifin-Rajka / UK Working Party criteria
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms the diagnosis based on chronicity, itch, morphology, distribution, and atopic history. Sensitivity 85-95% in research settings; remains the reference standard.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A clinician reviews medical and family history, asks about itch, sleep, triggers, and previous treatments, and examines the whole skin including scalp, hands, and flexures. No blood or imaging is required for typical cases.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies disease extent, intensity, and patient-reported impact to guide treatment intensity and monitor response. Required for biologic prescribing in most health systems.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 5-10 minute structured assessment by the clinician plus a brief patient questionnaire. Repeated at follow-up to track response — clinically meaningful improvement is typically a 50-75% drop in EASI.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects allergic contact dermatitis in adults and older children with chronic, treatment-resistant, or unusually distributed eczema. The European or North American Standard Series screens 30-80 allergens including metals, fragrances, preservatives, and rubber chemicals.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Allergen-loaded chambers are taped to the upper back for 48 hours, then read at days 2, 4 and 7. Patients avoid bathing the area, exercise, and sun during testing.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Performed for atypical or treatment-refractory disease to exclude cutaneous T-cell lymphoma (mycosis fungoides), psoriasis, dermatomyositis, or scabies. Histology shows spongiosis with perivascular lymphocytic infiltrate in eczema.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 4 mm punch biopsy under local anaesthetic, with 1-2 sutures, takes 10-15 minutes in clinic. Results return in 1-2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.test: Targeted IgE and skin-prick allergy testing
diagnosticTests.purpose: Reserved for infants and children with moderate-severe atopic dermatitis and a clinical history of immediate reactions to specific foods or aeroallergens, and for adult disease unresponsive to standard therapy. Broad untargeted panels are discouraged.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small drop of allergen is placed on the forearm and the skin is gently pricked; reactions are read at 15-20 minutes. Specific IgE is a single blood draw with results in 2-5 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Bacterial swab and culture (when infection suspected)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies Staphylococcus aureus, including methicillin-resistant strains, in oozing crusted lesions and guides targeted antibiotic therapy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A sterile swab is rolled over the lesion for a few seconds; culture results return in 24-72 hours. Mild stinging is the only discomfort.

treatmentOverview: Eczema treatment follows a stepwise approach codified by the American Academy of Dermatology (Sidbury / Davis 2024) and the EuroGuiDerm 2022 guideline. The foundation, applied at every severity, is daily skin-barrier care: liberal use of a thick fragrance-free emollient (ideally containing ceramides) two to three times a day, lukewarm short showers, gentle non-soap cleansers, and the soak-and-seal technique within three minutes of bathing. For mild flares, low-potency topical corticosteroids such as hydrocortisone 1-2.5% on face and intertriginous areas and mid-potency triamcinolone 0.1% on body sites quiet inflammation within days. Topical calcineurin inhibitors — tacrolimus 0.1% ointment and pimecrolimus 1% cream — are first-line steroid-sparing options for the face, eyelids, and folds. Newer non-steroid topicals include crisaborole 2% ointment (PDE-4 inhibitor), ruxolitinib 1.5% cream (topical JAK1/2 inhibitor, maximum 60 g per week), and tapinarof 1% cream (aryl hydrocarbon receptor agonist). Proactive maintenance therapy — applying topical corticosteroid or calcineurin inhibitor twice weekly to areas that previously flared — reduces relapse rates by roughly 50%. Wet-wrap therapy and dilute-bleach baths (a quarter cup of household bleach in a full tub, twice weekly) help severe or infection-prone disease. Antihistamines have a limited role: sedating agents at night may aid sleep, but non-sedating antihistamines do not relieve atopic itch. For moderate-to-severe disease that fails optimized topical therapy, AAD 2024 places dupilumab — a monoclonal antibody against the interleukin-4 receptor alpha chain (600 mg loading dose, then 300 mg every 2 weeks) — as the first-line systemic option for patients aged 6 months and older. Tralokinumab and lebrikizumab (anti-IL-13 biologics) and oral JAK inhibitors upadacitinib (15 or 30 mg daily) and abrocitinib (100 or 200 mg daily) are alternatives. Narrowband UVB phototherapy is a useful steroid-sparing option for widespread disease. Short-course cyclosporine (3-5 mg/kg/day) bridges severe flares but is not for long-term use; chronic systemic corticosteroids should be avoided because of rebound flares and toxicity.

medicalTreatments.name: Emollients with ceramides (apply 2-3 times daily; minimum 250 g per week in adults)
medicalTreatments.description: The foundation of all eczema care. Restores barrier lipids, reduces transepidermal water loss, and lowers the need for topical corticosteroids. Fragrance-free preparations with ceramides, cholesterol, and free fatty acids in a 3:1:1 ratio are most evidence-supported.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduce flare frequency by roughly 50% as monotherapy in mild disease and cut topical corticosteroid use by 30-50% across all severities.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical corticosteroids (hydrocortisone 1-2.5% face/folds; triamcinolone 0.1% body; clobetasol 0.05% for thick lichenified plaques, short courses only)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line anti-inflammatory therapy for flares. Potency selected by site (low on face and intertriginous areas, mid on body, high for lichenified plaques). Applied once or twice daily during a flare and twice weekly for proactive maintenance.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clearance or near-clearance in 70-80% of mild-moderate flares within 2-4 weeks; proactive twice-weekly use halves relapse rate.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus 0.1% ointment or 0.03% in children; pimecrolimus 1% cream)
medicalTreatments.description: Steroid-sparing immunomodulators ideal for face, eyelids, neck, and folds where chronic corticosteroid use causes atrophy. Apply twice daily during flares and twice weekly for proactive control. Mild transient burning is the main side effect.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Investigator Global Assessment clear or almost-clear in 50-60% at 6 weeks; long-term safety established over 10-year cohorts.

medicalTreatments.name: Crisaborole 2% ointment (apply twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Topical phosphodiesterase-4 inhibitor approved for mild-to-moderate atopic dermatitis in patients aged 3 months and older. Non-steroidal alternative for sensitive sites.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ISGA success in roughly 32-33% versus 21-25% with vehicle at day 29 (AD-301 / AD-302 pivotal trials).

medicalTreatments.name: Ruxolitinib 1.5% cream (apply twice daily; maximum 60 g per week, 20% body surface area)
medicalTreatments.description: Topical JAK1/JAK2 inhibitor for mild-to-moderate atopic dermatitis in patients aged 12 and older. Provides rapid itch relief, often within 12 hours of the first application.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: IGA-TS success in 53.8% at week 8 versus 15.1% with vehicle (TRuE-AD1 / TRuE-AD2).

medicalTreatments.name: Tapinarof 1% cream (apply once daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Aryl hydrocarbon receptor agonist that downregulates Th2 inflammation and supports barrier repair. Approved in atopic dermatitis in patients aged 2 years and older.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: vIGA-AD clear or almost-clear in roughly 45% at week 8 in pivotal trials, with durable remission off therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Dupilumab (300 mg subcutaneous every 2 weeks after a 600 mg loading dose; weight-based dosing in pediatrics from 6 months)
medicalTreatments.description: Monoclonal antibody against interleukin-4 receptor alpha, blocking IL-4 and IL-13 signalling. AAD 2024 first-line systemic for moderate-to-severe disease failing topical therapy. Conjunctivitis is the main adverse effect (10-20%); no laboratory monitoring required.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: EASI-75 in 51-69% and IGA 0/1 in 36-38% at week 16 (SOLO-1 and SOLO-2; Simpson NEJM 2016).

medicalTreatments.name: Tralokinumab (300 mg subcutaneous every 2 weeks after 600 mg load)
medicalTreatments.description: Monoclonal antibody against interleukin-13. Approved for adults and adolescents with moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis as an alternative to dupilumab.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: EASI-75 in 33-42% at week 16 in ECZTRA-1/2; comparable durability with continued dosing.

medicalTreatments.name: Upadacitinib (15 or 30 mg orally once daily) and abrocitinib (100 or 200 mg orally once daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Oral JAK1 inhibitors for moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis. Rapid itch control within days. Boxed warnings for serious infection, thrombosis, malignancy, and major cardiovascular events; baseline screening and ongoing monitoring required.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: EASI-75 in roughly 70-80% at week 16 with upadacitinib 30 mg and 60-70% with abrocitinib 200 mg (Guttman-Yassky JAMA Dermatol 2020; JADE trials).

medicalTreatments.name: Narrowband UVB phototherapy (typically 2-3 sessions per week for 8-16 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Steroid-sparing option for widespread moderate disease. Suppresses Th2 inflammation and reduces skin S. aureus colonization.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: EASI-50 in roughly 60% of patients within 12 weeks; non-systemic profile attractive for pregnancy and pediatric patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Cyclosporine (3-5 mg/kg/day orally, short-term)
medicalTreatments.description: Rapid-acting calcineurin inhibitor reserved as a bridge for severe flares while initiating a longer-term agent. Limited to courses of 3-6 months due to renal toxicity and hypertension.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Roughly 50% reduction in SCORAD within 6-8 weeks in randomized trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Dilute-bleach baths (¼ cup of household 6% bleach in a full tub, 2 times weekly, 10 minutes)
medicalTreatments.description: Adjunctive antimicrobial measure for patients with recurrent Staphylococcus aureus infection or moderate-severe disease. Reduces skin S. aureus load without driving resistance.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Roughly 35% reduction in EASI in randomized pediatric trials versus plain water bathing.

alternativeTreatments.name: Daily emollient prophylaxis from birth in high-risk infants
alternativeTreatments.description: Meta-analysis of randomized trials suggests a modest reduction in atopic dermatitis incidence at 6-12 months when emollients are applied daily from the first weeks of life in infants with a family history. Effect size is small (relative risk roughly 0.7-0.8) and not all trials replicate.

alternativeTreatments.name: Education and eczema action plans
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured eczema schools and written action plans improve adherence and reduce flares by roughly 30% across randomized pediatric trials. Pairing every prescription with a teach-back demonstration of fingertip-unit dosing is high-yield.

alternativeTreatments.name: Habit-reversal training and cognitive-behavioural therapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Targets the itch-scratch cycle in chronic disease. Modest but reproducible improvements in symptom scores and sleep, especially in adults with long-standing eczema.

whySeeSpecialist: Refer to dermatology when first-line topical therapy fails after 4-6 weeks, when more than 10% of body surface area is involved, when sleep is regularly disrupted, when there are repeated skin infections, when systemic therapy or phototherapy is being considered, or when there is diagnostic uncertainty versus psoriasis, cutaneous T-cell lymphoma, or scabies. Pediatric patients with moderate-severe disease benefit from combined dermatology-allergy review to address food allergy, asthma, and growth.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a board-certified dermatologist with experience prescribing biologics (dupilumab, tralokinumab) and oral JAK inhibitors, access to patch testing for atopic patients with possible contact allergy, and a clinic that uses validated severity tools (EASI, POEM, vIGA-AD) at every visit. Continuity matters — eczema is a long-game disease; a clinician who knows your trigger profile and treatment history will out-perform episodic specialist visits.
preventionStrategies: Apply a fragrance-free ceramide-rich emollient from the first weeks of life in infants with a parent or sibling with atopic disease — may modestly reduce eczema incidence
Bathe in lukewarm water for under 10 minutes using a gentle non-soap cleanser, then apply moisturizer within 3 minutes (the soak-and-seal method)
Identify and avoid established personal triggers — fragranced products, wool, dust mite, pet dander, harsh detergents — without resorting to broad untested elimination diets
Maintain home humidity around 40-50% during winter and use breathable cotton or bamboo clothing next to skin
Treat hand dermatitis early in wet-work occupations with gloves over cotton liners and protective emollient applied through the shift
Avoid tobacco smoke in pregnancy and the household; both raise offspring eczema risk by 15-20%
lifestyleModifications: Twice-daily emollient application even when skin looks clear — barrier dysfunction persists between flares
Keep fingernails short and consider cotton gloves at night to reduce sleep scratching damage
Wash new clothes before wearing, use fragrance-free detergent, and skip fabric softeners
Manage stress with sleep hygiene, regular exercise, and (where indicated) cognitive-behavioural therapy or habit-reversal training
Address comorbid asthma, allergic rhinitis, and food allergy with the relevant specialist — they share inflammatory drivers and control of one often improves the other
Track flares with a simple diary (food, weather, products, stress, sleep) to identify personal triggers
Discuss vaccination plans with the team before starting JAK inhibitors or other immunomodulators — live vaccines may need to be brought forward

recommended: Diverse balanced diet appropriate for age — no specific food group eliminations without documented allergy
Adequate vitamin D in deficient patients (serum 25-OH-D below 20 ng/mL) — supplementation modestly improves SCORAD in randomized trials
Omega-3-rich foods (oily fish, flaxseed) — modest anti-inflammatory effect in observational data
Probiotic-containing fermented foods if tolerated — most useful prenatally and in early infancy
Plenty of fluids to maintain hydration without affecting eczema directly
avoid: Broad untested elimination diets in children — they cause growth faltering and new food allergies (especially loss of tolerance to previously eaten foods)
Documented allergens confirmed by oral food challenge or clear immediate-reaction history
Foods that consistently flare an individual patient on a kept diary
Excess alcohol in adults — disrupts sleep and increases itch perception
Highly processed diets in children — associated with higher eczema severity in cohort studies

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise is encouraged and improves quality of life despite the irritant effect of sweat. Wear breathable cotton or moisture-wicking fabrics, shower lukewarm within an hour, and reapply emollient afterwards. Chlorinated pools are tolerated by most; rinse and moisturize immediately after swimming. Avoid prolonged hot showers and saunas during active flares.
dailyManagement: Apply a thick fragrance-free emollient at least twice daily — the single highest-yield habit in eczema care
Use the fingertip unit dosing method for topical corticosteroids to avoid under- or over-treatment
Bathe in lukewarm short showers with a gentle non-soap cleanser; pat skin damp, then seal with moisturizer
Keep nails short; consider cotton gloves at night for severe nocturnal scratching
Maintain a flare action plan: at the first sign of itch or redness, step up to the prescribed flare regimen rather than waiting
prognosis: Eczema is a chronic relapsing-remitting disease, but most childhood cases improve significantly with age — roughly 50-60% of children meet criteria for clinical remission by adolescence, and 30-40% have persistent or recurrent disease in adulthood. Predictors of persistence include early severe disease, filaggrin loss-of-function variants, sensitization to multiple allergens, and onset before 3 months of age. With current therapy, well-controlled patients achieve clear or almost-clear skin for most of the year, sleep well, and have minimal impact on school or work. Biologic-treated moderate-severe patients achieve EASI-75 in roughly 50-70% at week 16 and many maintain that response over years on continued dosing. Eczema itself does not shorten life, but the disease carries measurable burden: increased risk of depression (about 1.3-1.5 fold), anxiety, suicidal ideation in severe disease, growth impairment in poorly controlled children, and a 2-3 fold increased risk of cutaneous infection. The atopic march — progression from eczema to food allergy, asthma, and allergic rhinitis — affects 60-80% of moderate-severe pediatric patients, reinforcing the case for early effective barrier and inflammation control.
recoveryTimeline: An acute eczema flare typically responds within days of restarting topical corticosteroid or calcineurin inhibitor: visible redness drops within 3-5 days, itch within 5-10 days, and most flares resolve within 2-4 weeks. Lichenified plaques take longer — 4-12 weeks of consistent therapy plus emollient. Once dupilumab is started, half of responders see meaningful itch reduction within 2-4 weeks and peak response is reached around week 16; tralokinumab and lebrikizumab follow similar kinetics. Oral JAK inhibitors act fastest, with measurable itch relief in 24-48 hours. Proactive maintenance (twice-weekly topical anti-inflammatory plus daily emollient) is continued indefinitely to prevent relapse.
complications: Eczema herpeticum — disseminated herpes simplex virus infection of eczematous skin with painful punched-out vesicles, fever, and lymphadenopathy; dermatologic emergency requiring IV acyclovir
Bacterial superinfection, most commonly Staphylococcus aureus impetiginization, presenting as golden honey-coloured crust, oozing, and worsening flare
Molluscum contagiosum and widespread viral warts spread by scratching and barrier breakdown
Sleep disturbance from nocturnal itch — measurable cognitive and behavioural impact, including a 1.5-fold higher risk of ADHD diagnosis in severely affected children
Anxiety, depression, and suicidal ideation in severe adult disease (roughly 1.3-1.5 fold increased risk)
Growth impairment in children with severe poorly controlled disease, partly from chronic inflammation and partly from sleep disruption
Atopic march — sequential development of food allergy, asthma, and allergic rhinitis in 60-80% of moderate-severe pediatric patients
Significant financial and time burden — out-of-pocket costs in the US average roughly USD 600 per year for moderate disease and several thousand for biologic-treated severe disease

supportResources.name: National Eczema Association (NEA)
supportResources.description: US-based patient advocacy organization with evidence-based education, product seal-of-acceptance lists, and clinical-trial directory.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Dermatology — Atopic Dermatitis
supportResources.description: Authoritative US patient education from the body that publishes the AAD atopic dermatitis guidelines.

supportResources.name: Eczema Outreach Support (UK)
supportResources.description: UK charity supporting children and families with eczema; helpline, peer support, and school resources.

supportResources.name: DermNet — Atopic Dermatitis
supportResources.description: Detailed peer-reviewed clinical and patient-facing content from DermNet New Zealand, used by clinicians worldwide.

confusedWithConditions.name: Psoriasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Psoriasis presents with well-demarcated thick erythematous plaques topped with silvery scale on extensor surfaces (elbows, knees), scalp, and the lumbosacral area, while eczema favours flexural creases with ill-defined erythema and exudate. Psoriasis itch is generally milder, the Auspitz sign is present, and nail pitting and arthritis can accompany it.

confusedWithConditions.name: Scabies
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Scabies causes intense nocturnal itch with linear burrows in the finger webs, wrists, axillae, areolae, and genitals, often spreading among household members. Eczema lacks burrows, follows flexural patterns, and is non-contagious. Any new-onset severely itchy eruption with affected family contacts should prompt scabies treatment regardless of biopsy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Seborrheic Dermatitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Seborrheic dermatitis produces greasy yellow scale on the scalp, eyebrows, nasolabial folds, and chest — sebum-rich areas — driven by Malassezia. Atopic eczema spares the central face in adults, favours flexures, and is more pruritic. The two can coexist (head-and-neck dermatitis in atopic adults).

confusedWithConditions.name: Allergic Contact Dermatitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Allergic contact dermatitis is localized to the area of allergen contact (earlobes from nickel, eyelids from cosmetics, hands from rubber gloves) with sharply demarcated borders and flares with re-exposure. Patch testing identifies the culprit. Atopic eczema is more diffuse, flexural, and lifelong.

confusedWithConditions.name: Nummular Eczema
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Nummular eczema appears as coin-shaped plaques on the extremities and trunk, most often in adults with dry skin, and is frequently mistaken for tinea corporis (which has central clearing and KOH-positive scale) or plaque psoriasis. Atopic eczema by contrast favours flexures and has atopic stigmata.

relatedConditions.name: Atopic Dermatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: The dominant clinical form of eczema; this is the entity meant by 'eczema' in most clinical contexts.

relatedConditions.name: Asthma
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares Th2 inflammation; 30-50% of moderate-severe eczema patients develop asthma as part of the atopic march.

relatedConditions.name: Allergic Rhinitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Co-occurs in 40-60% of patients; third step of the atopic march after eczema and asthma.

relatedConditions.name: Food Allergy
relatedConditions.relevance: Roughly 30% of infants with moderate-severe eczema have IgE-mediated food allergy; barrier failure may drive cutaneous food sensitization.

relatedConditions.name: Psoriasis
relatedConditions.relevance: The leading differential for adult eczema; shares chronic inflammatory mechanisms but different cytokine drivers and treatment pathways.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Urticaria
relatedConditions.relevance: Both produce intense itch but urticarial wheals are transient (under 24 hours) and lack scale; both can coexist in atopic patients.

faqs.question: Is eczema curable?
faqs.answer: Eczema is not curable in the strict sense, but it is highly controllable. Most children outgrow the worst of their disease by adolescence (roughly 50-60% are in clinical remission by age 18), and adults with persistent disease can achieve clear or almost-clear skin on modern therapy. Daily moisturizing, trigger avoidance, topical anti-inflammatories, and (when needed) biologics such as dupilumab keep flares to a minimum and protect quality of life over the long term.

faqs.question: What causes eczema?
faqs.answer: Eczema is driven by a defective skin barrier (often involving filaggrin gene variants and ceramide deficiency) combined with an overactive type 2 immune response that releases interleukin-4, interleukin-13, and interleukin-31. Genetics set susceptibility; environmental triggers such as soaps, dust mite, dry air, stress, and Staphylococcus aureus colonization tip a stable barrier into an active flare.

faqs.question: What does eczema look like?
faqs.answer: Eczema produces dry, itchy, red, scaly patches that may weep clear fluid during flares and become thickened (lichenified) over time. In infants it affects cheeks, scalp, and outer arms and legs; in older children and adults it favours the inner elbows, behind the knees, neck, wrists, and ankles. The skin between flares is typically dry, rough, and sensitive.

faqs.question: How is eczema diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Eczema is diagnosed clinically by a dermatologist or primary-care clinician using the Hanifin-Rajka or UK Working Party criteria — an itchy condition plus features such as flexural involvement, atopic history, early onset, and chronic dry skin. No blood test confirms it. Patch testing is used when allergic contact dermatitis is suspected, and biopsy is reserved for atypical or treatment-resistant adult cases.

faqs.question: What is the best moisturizer for eczema?
faqs.answer: The best moisturizers are thick, fragrance-free, and contain ceramides, cholesterol, and free fatty acids that replicate the natural skin barrier. Ointments and creams outperform lotions. Apply within three minutes of bathing and at least twice a day, even on skin that looks clear. Brands carrying the National Eczema Association Seal of Acceptance meet most evidence criteria.

faqs.question: Are steroid creams safe for eczema?
faqs.answer: Yes, topical corticosteroids are safe and effective when used correctly. Match potency to the site (low on face and folds, mid on body, high for thick plaques), use the fingertip-unit dosing method, and apply once or twice daily during flares. Long-term twice-weekly proactive use is also safe and halves relapse rates. Atrophy, stretch marks, and rosacea-like reactions occur only with prolonged misuse, particularly of high-potency steroids on the face.

faqs.question: What is dupilumab and who is it for?
faqs.answer: Dupilumab is a monoclonal antibody that blocks the interleukin-4 receptor alpha chain, shutting down IL-4 and IL-13 signalling. It is the first-line systemic therapy for moderate-to-severe eczema in patients aged 6 months and older whose disease fails optimized topical therapy. It is given as a subcutaneous injection every 2 weeks. About half of treated patients achieve a 75% improvement in EASI scores within 16 weeks.

faqs.question: Will my baby outgrow eczema?
faqs.answer: Most do. About half to 60% of children with infant-onset eczema have substantially improved or clear skin by adolescence. Predictors of persistence include severe early disease, multiple food and environmental sensitizations, and filaggrin gene variants. Early consistent treatment may also reduce the risk of progressing to asthma and hay fever.

faqs.question: Is eczema contagious?
faqs.answer: No, eczema itself is not contagious. You cannot catch it from skin contact or sharing towels. Secondary infections that complicate eczema — bacterial impetigo, herpes simplex (eczema herpeticum), molluscum contagiosum — can spread to others, so cover oozing or vesicular areas and seek treatment for sudden worsening.

faqs.question: What foods make eczema worse?
faqs.answer: For most patients, food is not the main driver. About 30% of infants with moderate-severe eczema have a true IgE-mediated food allergy (commonly to milk, egg, peanut, wheat, or soy), which can flare the skin within minutes to hours of exposure. Broad elimination diets without confirmed allergy worsen growth and can cause loss of food tolerance — avoid them unless directed by an allergist.

faqs.question: How do I stop the itch?
faqs.answer: Treat the inflammation first — a topical corticosteroid or calcineurin inhibitor cuts itch within days. Cool compresses, fragrance-free moisturizer, short cotton gloves at night, and shorter lukewarm showers all help. Non-sedating antihistamines do not relieve atopic itch but sedating ones may aid sleep. For severe itch, dupilumab and oral JAK inhibitors reduce itch within 24-48 hours.

faqs.question: Does stress cause eczema?
faqs.answer: Stress does not cause eczema but is a major flare trigger. Stress releases neuropeptides that lower the itch threshold and disrupt the barrier, while sleep loss amplifies the itch-scratch cycle. Habit-reversal training, cognitive-behavioural therapy, and sleep hygiene reduce flare frequency in chronic disease.

faqs.question: What's the difference between eczema and psoriasis?
faqs.answer: Eczema is intensely itchy, ill-defined, and oozes in flares, favouring flexural creases such as the inner elbows and behind the knees. Psoriasis presents as well-demarcated thick red plaques with silvery scale on extensor surfaces, scalp, and lumbosacral area, with milder itch and often nail or joint involvement. A dermatologist can usually tell them apart on examination, and biopsy resolves rare overlap.

faqs.question: Can adults develop eczema for the first time?
faqs.answer: Yes. Adult-onset atopic dermatitis is recognized in 1-3% of adults and often presents on the hands, eyelids, and head and neck. Triggers, contact allergens, and occupational exposures should be carefully reviewed. Treatment ladder is the same as in childhood disease, with biologics and JAK inhibitors widely used.

faqs.question: Should I use bleach baths?
faqs.answer: Dilute-bleach baths can help patients with moderate-severe eczema or frequent skin infections. Add a quarter cup of 6% household bleach to a full bathtub of lukewarm water, soak for 10 minutes, twice weekly, then rinse and moisturize. Randomized trials show roughly a 35% reduction in EASI in children prone to infection. Skip the bleach bath if skin is acutely fissured.

faqs.question: Are eczema and asthma related?
faqs.answer: Yes. Both are part of the atopic march, with shared type 2 inflammation. About 30-50% of children with moderate-severe eczema develop asthma, and 40-60% develop allergic rhinitis. Effective eczema control in early childhood may reduce later atopic progression, though this is still under study.

faqs.question: How much does eczema treatment cost?
faqs.answer: Generic emollients, topical corticosteroids, and calcineurin inhibitors are inexpensive — often under USD 50 a month combined. Biologics such as dupilumab and oral JAK inhibitors run several thousand dollars per month at US list price but are widely covered by insurance for moderate-severe disease meeting guideline criteria. In India and most emerging markets, allopurinol-tier generic topical therapy is widely affordable while biologic access is improving.

faqs.question: Can pregnant women treat eczema safely?
faqs.answer: Yes. Daily emollients, low-to-mid potency topical corticosteroids on small areas, and narrowband UVB phototherapy are considered safe in pregnancy. Topical calcineurin inhibitors are generally avoided. Dupilumab has reassuring registry data and may be continued in moderate-severe disease after discussion with a dermatologist. Oral JAK inhibitors are avoided in pregnancy.

faqs.question: When is eczema a medical emergency?
faqs.answer: Seek same-day care if you develop sudden painful clustered vesicles with fever (possible eczema herpeticum), spreading honey-coloured crusting and fever (bacterial superinfection), generalized peeling and chills (erythroderma), or eye pain and vision change. These complications can progress rapidly and need prompt antiviral, antibiotic, or hospital care.

faqs.question: Does climate or weather affect eczema?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cold dry winter air lowers skin humidity and worsens eczema; hot humid weather increases sweat and irritation; sudden temperature changes destabilize the barrier. Maintaining indoor humidity around 40-50%, wearing breathable cotton, and showering lukewarm after sweat exposure all help reduce weather-driven flares.

faqs.question: Can I exercise with eczema?
faqs.answer: Yes, and you should. Regular exercise improves quality of life and overall health. Wear moisture-wicking fabrics, shower lukewarm within an hour, and reapply emollient afterwards. Chlorinated pools are tolerated by most patients if you rinse and moisturize immediately. Avoid prolonged hot showers and saunas during active flares.

metaDescription: Eczema: intensely itchy chronic skin inflammation driven by barrier failure and Th2 immunity. Symptoms, treatment, and prognosis reviewed by our medical board.
